title: Burns (fire, hot water, chemical)
steps: Move the person away from the heat or chemical source. Ensure your own safety first.
For thermal burns, cool the area under clean running water for at least 10 minutes. Do not use ice.
Remove tight items near the burn (rings, watches) before swelling starts, unless stuck to skin.
Do not apply toothpaste, butter, shea butter, or raw egg — they trap heat and can cause infection.
Cover loosely with a clean, non-fluffy cloth or sterile dressing.
For chemical burns, brush off dry powder first, then rinse with lots of water. Remove contaminated clothing.
If the burn is larger than the person’s palm, on the face/hands/genitals, deep, or caused by chemicals or electricity, go to the hospital immediately.
If the person is in severe pain, weak, or having trouble breathing, seek emergency care.

title: Choking (adult and child)
steps: Encourage coughing if the person can cough, speak, or breathe — stay with them.
If they cannot breathe, speak, or cough effectively, call for help and start first aid.
For adults and children over 1 year: stand behind them, place a fist above the belly button, grasp with your other hand, give up to 5 sharp inward-upward thrusts (Heimlich).
If still blocked, give up to 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades, then repeat thrusts.
For infants under 1 year: give 5 back blows while supporting the head, then 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on the breastbone — repeat if needed.
If the person becomes unresponsive, begin CPR if you are trained and help them breathe when possible.
Even if the object clears, seek medical care if breathing was difficult or there is throat pain.

title: Bleeding / open wounds
steps: Wash your hands if possible or use gloves. Protect yourself from blood.
Apply firm, direct pressure on the wound with a clean cloth or gauze. Keep pressure steady.
Raise the injured part above heart level if it does not cause more pain or obvious fracture.
Do not remove material deeply stuck in the wound — pad around it and press.
Once bleeding slows, bandage snugly but not so tight that fingers or toes turn blue or numb.
Seek urgent care if bleeding does not stop in 10 minutes, spurts, the person feels faint, or the wound is deep, gaping, or caused by a dirty object.
Animal bites and deep dirty wounds may need tetanus update and medical cleaning — go to a facility.

title: Snake bites
steps: Keep the person calm and still. Panic speeds venom spread.
Call for emergency transport or go to the nearest hospital that stocks antivenom if available.
Remove tight jewellery or clothing near the bite before swelling.
Keep the bitten limb at or slightly below heart level. Do not let them run or walk long distances.
Do not cut, suck, apply a tourniquet, ice, or herbal pastes — these worsen tissue damage.
Note snake colour/pattern only if safe — a photo helps doctors, but do not delay leaving dangerous ground.
Monitor breathing and consciousness while travelling to care.

title: Drowning (rescue and first response)
steps: Shout for help. Only enter the water if you can do so safely — use a rope, pole, or flotation device if possible.
Get the person out of the water as quickly and safely as you can.
Check responsiveness and breathing. If unresponsive but breathing, place in recovery position and monitor.
If not breathing normally, call for help and start rescue breaths and chest compressions if you are trained (CPR).
Even if they seem fine after coughing up water, they still need medical assessment — “dry drowning” complications can appear later.
Keep the person warm with dry clothes or blankets after rescue.
All drowning survivors should be evaluated at a hospital.

title: Seizures / convulsions
steps: Stay calm. Note the time the seizure started.
Ease the person to the floor. Clear nearby sharp or hard objects.
Place something soft under the head. Loosen tight clothing around the neck.
Do not put anything in the mouth — they cannot swallow their tongue.
Do not restrain their movements. Turn them onto one side after jerking stops if breathing is OK.
Call emergency services if the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, repeats without full recovery, happens in pregnancy, or if it is their first seizure.
Afterwards, stay with them. Speak calmly. They may be confused — help them rest in a safe place.

title: Fainting / unconsciousness
steps: If they feel faint, have them sit or lie down and raise their legs above heart level if possible.
If unconscious, check responsiveness and breathing. Call for help.
If breathing normally, place in recovery position (on side, hand under cheek, leg bent).
If not breathing normally, begin CPR if trained.
Loosen tight belts or collars. Protect the airway. Do not give food or drink until fully alert.
If they do not wake within a minute, have unusual breathing, chest pain, head injury, or are pregnant, seek emergency care.
When awake, have them sit up slowly and sip water if dehydration might be the cause.

title: Bone fractures / sprains
steps: Keep the injured area still. Do not force a bent limb straight.
Apply ice wrapped in cloth for up to 15 minutes to reduce swelling (if skin is intact).
For suspected fracture, splint in the position found using boards, rolled newspaper, or a sling — immobilize joints above and below.
Treat open fractures: cover wound with clean dressing, do not push bone back, control bleeding with pressure.
Elevate the limb if it does not worsen pain. Watch fingers/toes for colour and feeling.
Go to the hospital for X-rays. Do not eat or drink if surgery might be needed — follow local advice.
For ankle/knee sprains: rest, ice, compression bandage, and elevation; seek care if they cannot bear weight or bone looks deformed.

title: Electric shock
steps: Do not touch the person if they are still in contact with the electrical source — you may be shocked too.
Turn off the power at the mains, unplug, or use a dry non-conductive object (wood, plastic) to separate them from the source.
Call emergency services. High-voltage lines: stay far away and call the power company.
Once safe to touch, check breathing and pulse. Start CPR if needed and you are trained.
Cool any burns with running water after the person is disconnected from power.
All electric shock victims should be medically evaluated — internal injuries may not be visible.
Watch for chest pain, confusion, or irregular heartbeat on the way to care.

title: Poisoning / food poisoning
steps: Find out what was taken, how much, and when. Keep containers or labels for hospital staff.
If the person is drowsy, having seizures, or trouble breathing, call emergency services immediately.
Do not try to induce vomiting unless a health professional tells you to — some substances cause more damage coming back up.
For swallowed chemicals, follow container instructions if safe; when unsure, seek phone advice or go to hospital.
For mild food poisoning with vomiting/diarrhea: sip oral rehydration or clean boiled water; rest.
Seek urgent care for blood in vomit/stool, high fever, severe belly pain, signs of dehydration, or vulnerable people (babies, elderly, pregnant).
Bring a sample or photo of the suspected poison or meal.

title: Allergic reactions
steps: Mild reaction (rash, itch, sneezing): help them sit, loosen tight clothing, and use any prescribed antihistamine they already have from a doctor.
Watch for swelling of lips/tongue, wheezing, hoarse voice, dizziness, or fainting — these are emergencies.
If they have prescribed adrenaline (epinephrine auto-injector), help them use it in the outer thigh and call emergency services.
Lay them flat with legs raised if faint, unless breathing is easier sitting upright.
Do not give food or drink if swallowing is difficult.
Stay with them until help arrives.
After any severe reaction, they must go to hospital for observation even if they feel better.

title: Fever in children
steps: Dress lightly. Offer frequent small drinks of water or oral rehydration. Do not bundle heavily.
If a doctor has advised paracetamol or ibuprofen for this child, give the correct dose for age/weight — never adult dosing.
Sponge with lukewarm water if uncomfortable; avoid cold baths or alcohol rubs.
Watch for danger signs: floppy or hard to wake, purple spots, stiff neck, non-stop crying, breathing difficulty, seizure, or not passing urine.
Babies under 3 months with any fever need prompt medical assessment.
Seek care if fever lasts more than 3 days, is very high, or the child refuses fluids.
Trust your instincts — if something feels very wrong, go to the hospital.

title: Nosebleeds
steps: Sit upright and lean slightly forward — not backward (swallowing blood can upset the stomach).
Pinch the soft part of the nose (below the bridge) firmly for 10–15 minutes without letting go to check early.
Breathe through the mouth. Spit out blood instead of swallowing when possible.
Apply a cold cloth to the nose bridge or cheeks if helpful.
After bleeding stops, avoid blowing the nose, heavy lifting, or hot drinks for several hours.
Seek care if bleeding lasts more than 20 minutes, is very heavy, follows injury, or happens often.
If the person feels faint, help them lie down with legs raised and get medical help.
